name: yxmath
documentation:
# yxmath
Xu Yuxing's personal math tools

functions.name: yxmath.set.merge_same_element_set_with_group_id
documentation:
merge sets which have at least one same element
\:param input_dict:

> input_dict = {
>
> : "a" : \[1,2,3,4,5\],
>   "b" : \[10,2,30,40,50\],
>   "c" : \[100,200,300,40,500\],
>   "d" : \[900,1000\]
>   }

```
return

output_list = [(a,b),(c)]
```

functions.name: yxmath.set.merge_same_element_set_with_group_id_by_graphs
documentation:
merge sets which have at least one same element
based on Connected component in graphs
\:param input_dict:

> input_dict = {
>
> : "a" : \[1,2,3,4,5\],
>   "b" : \[10,2,30,40,50\],
>   "c" : \[100,200,300,40,500\],
>   "d" : \[900,1000\]
>   }

```
return

output_list = [(a,b,c),(d)]
```

functions.name: yxmath.stats.t_test
documentation:
based on pingouin
see: https://pingouin-stats.org/generated/pingouin.ttest.html#pingouin.ttest
:return:
pandas.DataFrame

'T' : T-value
'p-val' : p-value
'dof' : degrees of freedom
'cohen-d' : Cohen d effect size
'CI95%' : 95% confidence intervals of the difference in means
'power' : achieved power of the test ( = 1 - type II error)
'BF10' : Bayes Factor of the alternative hypothesis

example:
    output = t_test([1,2,3,4,5,6,7],[7,6,1,4,3,5,6,4])

p_value = float(output['p-val'])
we use https://github.com/raphaelvallat/pingouin
t test

functions.name: yxmath.stats.two_categories_chi2
documentation:
ref:
<https://codingdisciple.com/chi-squared-python.html>
<https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.stats.chi2_contingency.html>
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contingency_table>

```
param Contingency_table

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contingency_table

if we have:

A1          A2

source:11: (WARNING/2) Block quote ends without a blank line; unexpected unindent.

B1  num1        num2        num1+num2
B2  num3        num4        num3+num4

source:13: (ERROR/3) Unexpected indentation.

num1+num3   num2+num4   num1+num2+num3+num4

then input Contingency_table will be:

Contingency_table = [[num1,num2],[num3,num4]]

return

```
chi2 test

B1  num1        num2        num1+num2
B2  num3        num4        num3+num4

functions.name: yxmath.stats.hypergeom_enrichment
documentation:
ref:
<https://stackoverflow.com/questions/6594840/what-are-equivalents-to-rs-phyper-function-in-python>
<http://mengnote.blogspot.com/2012/12/calculate-correct-hypergeometric-p.html>
<https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy-0.14.0/reference/generated/scipy.stats.hypergeom.html>
Zhou, P., et al. (2019). "Dynamic Patterns of Gene Expression Additivity and Regulatory Variation throughout Maize Development." Mol Plant 12(3): 410-425.

Models drawing objects from a bin. M is total number of objects, n is total number of Type I objects. RV counts number of Type I objects in N drawn without replacement from population.

```
param Contingency_table

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contingency_table

if we have:

A1          A2

source:14: (WARNING/2) Block quote ends without a blank line; unexpected unindent.

B1  num1        num2        num1+num2
B2  num3        num4        num3+num4

source:16: (ERROR/3) Unexpected indentation.

num1+num3   num2+num4   num1+num2+num3+num4

then input Contingency_table will be:

Contingency_table = [[num1,num2],[num3,num4]]

return

```

functions.name: yxmath.stats.two_distribution_test
documentation:
---
param data1: list for all data from set 1
param data2: list for all data from set 2
return: KS statistic (D statistic) and p-value
---

If the K-S statistic is small or the p-value is high, then we cannot reject the hypothesis that the distributions of the two samples are the same.

<https://stats.idre.ucla.edu/stata/faq/how-can-i-test-for-equality-of-distribution/>
HOW CAN I TEST FOR EQUALITY OF DISTRIBUTION?
An alternative test to the classic t-test is the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test for equality of distribution functions.
In a simple example, we’ll see if the distribution of writing test scores across gender are equal using the High-School
and Beyond 2000 data set. We’ll first do a kernel density plot of writing scores by gender.

<https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy-0.14.0/reference/generated/scipy.stats.ks_2samp.html>

file_list = \[
'/lustre/home/xuyuxing/Liu/A_D_Arabidopsis_F.txt',
'/lustre/home/xuyuxing/Liu/A_D_Arabidopsis_O.txt',
'/lustre/home/xuyuxing/Liu/A_D_Dodder_F.txt',
'/lustre/home/xuyuxing/Liu/A_D_Dodder_O.txt',
'/lustre/home/xuyuxing/Liu/S_D_Dodder_F.txt',
'/lustre/home/xuyuxing/Liu/S_D_Dodder_O.txt',
'/lustre/home/xuyuxing/Liu/S_D_Soybean_F.txt',
'/lustre/home/xuyuxing/Liu/S_D_Soybean_O.txt',
\]

from toolbiox.lib.common.fileIO import read_list_file

data_list = \[\]
for i in file_list:

> data_list.append(\[float(j) for j in read_list_file(i) if j != ''\])

for i in \[0,2,4,6\]:

: print(two_distribution_test(data_list\[i\],data_list\[i+1\]))

functions.name: yxmath.stats.anova
documentation:
see https://pingouin-stats.org/generated/pingouin.anova.html#pingouin.anova for detail

aov = pg.anova(dv='len', between=['supp', 'dose'], data=input_dataframe, detailed=True)

input_dataframe should be pandas dataframe, each column is a variable, first row is name of variable for the column
if in a csv file, it should like 'https://vincentarelbundock.github.io/Rdatasets/csv/datasets/ToothGrowth.csv'
make df as:
data = 'https://vincentarelbundock.github.io/Rdatasets/csv/datasets/ToothGrowth.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(data, index_col=0)

dv is one of the variable name which as y

between is list of variable name

:return
'Source' : Factor names
'SS' : Sums of squares
'DF' : Degrees of freedom
'MS' : Mean squares
'F' : F-values
'p-unc' : uncorrected p-values
'np2' : Partial eta-square effect sizes
anova
https://www.analyticsvidhya.com/blog/2018/01/anova-analysis-of-variance/
https://pingouin-stats.org/generated/pingouin.anova.html#pingouin.anova

modules.name: yxmath.lcs
documentation: ## solve the longest common subsequence problem
get the matrix of LCS lengths at each sub-step of the recursive process
(m+1 by n+1, where m=len(list1) & n=len(list2) ... it's one larger in each direction
so we don't have to special-case the x-1 cases at the first elements of the iteration

functions.name: yxmath.lcs.all_lcs
documentation: backtracks all the LCSs through a provided matrix

functions.name: yxmath.lcs.lcs
documentation: return a set of the sets of longest common subsequences in list1 and list2

documentation:

base_translate(21,2)

functions.name: yxmath.split.cover_windows_index
documentation:
For a known windows set for a sequence (given step length, windows length, and start site).
Now when I am on a position(pos), which windows covered me ?=
\:param pos: Pos site I am
\:param step: step for windows
\:param window: window length for windows
\:param start: where is start for first window
\:return: windows index (begin for 0) for windows which covered me
windows

functions.name: yxmath.split.windows_index_to_range
documentation:
For a known windows set for a sequence (given step length, windows length, and start site).
Now I give a index (begin for 0), give me the detail for this window
\:param pos: index I have
\:param step: step for windows
\:param window: window length for windows
\:param start: where is start for first window
\:return: windows start and end

functions.name: yxmath.split.total_windows
documentation:
return how many windows can be get for a given sequence and given windows parameter
\:param end: sequence length or end for window split
\:param step: step for windows
\:param window: windows length
\:param start: where is the start for the first window, default: 1
\:return: windows number

functions.name: yxmath.split.split_sequence_to_windows
documentation:
make a windows split for a sequence
\:param end: sequence length or end for window split
\:param step: step for windows
\:param window: windows length
\:param start: where is the start for the first window, default 1
\:return: generator for windows index, start and end

functions.name: yxmath.split.cover_bin_index
documentation:
For a known bins set for a sequence (given bin length and start site).
Now when I am on a position(pos), which bin covered me ?
\:param pos: Pos site I am
\:param bin_length: length for a bin
\:param start: where is the start for the first bin
\:return: bin index (begin for 0) for windows which covered me
bin (means windows without overlap)

functions.name: yxmath.split.bin_index_to_range
documentation:
For a known bins set for a sequence (given bin_length, and start site).
Now I give a index (begin for 0), give me the detail for this bin
\:param index: index I have
\:param bin_length: length of each bin
\:param start: where is start for first bin
\:return: bin start and end

functions.name: yxmath.split.total_bins
documentation:
return how many bin can be get for a given bin_length
\:param end: sequence length or end for bin split
\:param bin_length: length of each bin
\:param start: where is the start for the first bin, default: 1
\:return: bins number

functions.name: yxmath.split.split_sequence_to_bins
documentation:
make a windows split for a sequence
\:param end: sequence length or end for window split
\:param step: step for windows
\:param window: windows length
\:param start: where is the start for the first window, default 1
\:return: generator for windows index, start and end

modules.name: yxmath.plot.VennDiagram
documentation: coding: utf-8

functions.name: yxmath.plot.VennDiagram.get_labels
documentation:
get a dict of labels for groups in data
@type data: list[Iterable]
@rtype: dict[str, str]
input
  data: data to get label for
  fill: ["number"|"logic"|"percent"]
return
  labels: a dict of labels for different sets
example:
In [12]: get_labels([range(10), range(5,15), range(3,8)], fill=["number"])
Out[12]:
{'001': '0',
 '010': '5',
 '011': '0',
 '100': '3',
 '101': '2',
 '110': '2',
 '111': '3'}

functions.name: yxmath.plot.VennDiagram.venn2
documentation:
plots a 2-set Venn diagram
@type labels: dict[str, str]
@type names: list[str]
@rtype: (Figure, AxesSubplot)
input
  labels: a label dict where keys are identified via binary codes ('01', '10', '11'),
          hence a valid set could look like: {'01': 'text 1', '10': 'text 2', '11': 'text 3'}.
          unmentioned codes are considered as ''.
  names:  group names
  more:   colors, figsize, dpi, fontsize
return
  pyplot Figure and AxesSubplot object

functions.name: yxmath.plot.VennDiagram.venn3
documentation:
plots a 3-set Venn diagram
@type labels: dict[str, str]
@type names: list[str]
@rtype: (Figure, AxesSubplot)
input
  labels: a label dict where keys are identified via binary codes ('001', '010', '100', ...),
          hence a valid set could look like: {'001': 'text 1', '010': 'text 2', '100': 'text 3', ...}.
          unmentioned codes are considered as ''.
  names:  group names
  more:   colors, figsize, dpi, fontsize
return
  pyplot Figure and AxesSubplot object

functions.name: yxmath.plot.VennDiagram.venn4
documentation:
plots a 4-set Venn diagram
@type labels: dict[str, str]
@type names: list[str]
@rtype: (Figure, AxesSubplot)
input
  labels: a label dict where keys are identified via binary codes ('0001', '0010', '0100', ...),
          hence a valid set could look like: {'0001': 'text 1', '0010': 'text 2', '0100': 'text 3', ...}.
          unmentioned codes are considered as ''.
  names:  group names
  more:   colors, figsize, dpi, fontsize
return
  pyplot Figure and AxesSubplot object

functions.name: yxmath.plot.VennDiagram.venn5
documentation:
plots a 5-set Venn diagram
@type labels: dict[str, str]
@type names: list[str]
@rtype: (Figure, AxesSubplot)
input
  labels: a label dict where keys are identified via binary codes ('00001', '00010', '00100', ...),
          hence a valid set could look like: {'00001': 'text 1', '00010': 'text 2', '00100': 'text 3', ...}.
          unmentioned codes are considered as ''.
  names:  group names
  more:   colors, figsize, dpi, fontsize
return
  pyplot Figure and AxesSubplot object

functions.name: yxmath.plot.VennDiagram.venn6
documentation:
plots a 6-set Venn diagram
@type labels: dict[str, str]
@type names: list[str]
@rtype: (Figure, AxesSubplot)
input
  labels: a label dict where keys are identified via binary codes ('000001', '000010', '000100', ...),
          hence a valid set could look like: {'000001': 'text 1', '000010': 'text 2', '000100': 'text 3', ...}.
          unmentioned codes are considered as ''.
  names:  group names
  more:   colors, figsize, dpi, fontsize
return
  pyplot Figure and AxesSubplot object
